sections.paragraph:
m.-

(1) a pomegranate, Son 4:3 also an artificial one, Exo 28:33, 34 Exo 28:34 2Ki 25:17 a pomegranate tree, Joe 1:12. (Arabic رُمَّانُ id. The origin is doubtful. Some have supposed, very improbably, that pomegranates were so called from the worms (רִמָּה) with which they are infested. I prefer explaining רִמֹּון marrowy, from رِمُّ marrow of a bone, رمّ to be marrowy as a bone.)

From the abundance of pomegranates, several places take their names-(a) [Rimmon, Remmon], a town of the Simeonites, on the southern confines of Palestine, Jos 15:32 19:7 Zec 14:10.-(b) a town of the Zebulonites, Jos 19:13 (where הַמְּתֹאָר does not belong to the pr.n., see under the word תָּאַר Pual), perhaps i.q. רִמֹּונֹו 1Ch 6:62.-(c) of a rock near Gibeah, Jud. 20:45 , 47 Jdg 20:47, to which some also refer 1Sa 14:2 [prob. now called Rŭmmôn, رَمّونَ Rob. ii. 113].-(d) רִמֹּון פֶּרֶץ [Rimmon-perez], a station of the Israelites, Num 33:19.

(2) pr.n. of an idol of the Syrians, 2Ki 5:18 (compare pr.n. טַבְרִמֹּון), perhaps high, from the root רָמַם No. 1. Hesych. Ῥαμάς, ὕφιστος θεός. Hence pr.n. of a man, 2Sa 4:2.
